original_abstract:
This study investigated the impact of various solvent systems on the extraction of antioxidant compounds from black mulberry (*Morus nigra*), blackberry (*Rubus ulmifolius*), and strawberry (*Fragaria x ananassa*). To determine the most effective extraction methods, we analyzed different fruit extracts for total phenolic content, total anthocyanins, and overall antioxidant capacity (using FRAP, DPPH•, and ORAC assays). Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was subsequently employed to provide a comprehensive overview of how solvent choice influences extraction efficiency and to identify patterns among the diverse treatments.

Our results indicate that a 70% acetone/30% water (v/v) mixture was the most effective solvent for recovering phenolic compounds across all berries. In contrast, the highest anthocyanin yields were consistently achieved using a acidified methanol mixture (methanol/water/acetic acid, 70:29.5:0.5, v/v/v). The optimal solvent for maximizing antioxidant capacity varied by fruit: 50% ethanol was best for black mulberries, acidified 70% acetone for blackberries, and 50% acetone for strawberries. Overall, black mulberry exhibited the highest concentrations of bioactive compounds. PCA successfully distinguished the superior performance of organic solvent-water mixtures over pure organic solvents, confirming that solvent hydration and polarity are critical factors. We conclude that the extraction of berry antioxidants is highly sensitive to the specific solvent combination used, necessitating tailored approaches for different classes of phytochemicals.
humanized_abstract:
In this work the effect of different solvent systems was investigated for the recovery of antioxidants from black mulberry (Morus nigra), blackberry (Rubus ulmifolius) and strawberry (Fragaria x ananassa). By determining the levels of the total phenolic content, total anthocyanins and their respective antioxidant activity, measured through FRAP, DPPH• and ORAC assays, on different fruit extracts, effective extraction procedures were demonstrated. By applying the Principal Component Analysis (PCA) technique the effect of different solvent systems on the amounts of extracted antioxidants, as well as the pattern of the studied treatments were depicted in a 3 dimensional space.

The most effective solvent for the extraction of phenolic compounds from all berries was found to be 70% acetone/30% water (v/v) and the highest amount of anthocyanins were extracted using acidified methanol (methanol/water/acetic acid 70:29.5:0.5 v/v/v). Different optimised solvents were required to extract antioxidants from differing fruits; 50% ethanol for black mulberries, acidified 70% acetone for blackberries and 50% acetone for strawberries. Bioactive compound content was highest in black mulberries. PCA confirmed that the organic solvent-water mixtures generally gave better extraction than the pure organic solvent; and that hydration and polarity of the solvent affected the extraction of berry antioxidants. The results indicate that extraction of berry antioxidants is highly dependent on the chosen solvent combination and that different solvents are required to achieve optimal extraction of different phytochemicals.

The most effective solvent for the extraction of phenolic compounds from all berries was found to be 70% acetone/30% water (v/v) and the highest amount of anthocyanins were extracted using acidified methanol (methanol/water/acetic acid 70:29.5:0.5 v/v/v). document.input:
This study investigated the impact of various solvent systems on the extraction of antioxidant compounds from black mulberry (*Morus nigra*), blackberry (*Rubus ulmifolius*), and strawberry (*Fragaria x ananassa*). To determine the most effective extraction methods, we analyzed different fruit extracts for total phenolic content, total anthocyanins, and overall antioxidant capacity (using FRAP, DPPH•, and ORAC assays). PCA successfully distinguished the superior performance of organic solvent-water mixtures over pure organic solvents, confirming that solvent hydration and polarity are critical factors. We conclude that the extraction of berry antioxidants is highly sensitive to the specific solvent combination used, necessitating tailored approaches for different classes of phytochemicals.
